flashcards.front: What is the formal definition of a parallel computer according to the textbook?
flashcards.back: A parallel computer is a collection of processing elements that cooperate to solve large problems faster by working concurrently on different parts of the problem.

flashcards.front: Define SISD (Single Instruction Single Data) in Flynn's Taxonomy
flashcards.back: SISD represents the traditional sequential computer architecture where a single processor executes a single instruction stream on a single data stream. Examples: uniprocessor workstations, early personal computers.

flashcards.front: Define SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data) in Flynn's Taxonomy
flashcards.back: SIMD architecture has multiple processing elements that execute the same instruction stream simultaneously on different data streams. Examples: vector processors, GPU arrays, Intel SSE/AVX units.

flashcards.front: Define MIMD (Multiple Instruction Multiple Data) in Flynn's Taxonomy
flashcards.back: MIMD systems have multiple independent processors that execute different instructions on different data streams. Examples: modern multi-core CPUs, distributed memory clusters, supercomputers.

flashcards.front: Define MISD (Multiple Instruction Single Data) in Flynn's Taxonomy
flashcards.back: MISD has multiple processors executing different instructions on the same data stream. This category is rare in practice; examples include systolic arrays and certain fault-tolerant systems.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Uniform Memory Access (UMA) and Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA)?
flashcards.back: In UMA, all processors have equal access time to any memory location through a shared bus or interconnect. In NUMA, memory access time depends on the distance between processor and memory; local memory is faster than remote memory.

flashcards.front: What is the key characteristic of distributed memory architectures?
flashcards.back: Each processor has its own private memory, and processors communicate via an interconnection network by passing messages. Memory access is not uniform, and communication is explicit through message passing.

flashcards.front: What does SPMD stand for and what does it mean?
flashcards.back: SPMD (Single Program Multiple Data) is an execution model where all processors execute the same program but operate on different data sets. Each process may take different execution paths based on its rank/ID.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between data parallelism and task parallelism?
flashcards.back: Data parallelism involves applying the same operation to different data elements concurrently (SIMD-like). Task parallelism involves distributing different tasks/operations to different processors, which may execute simultaneously.

flashcards.front: State Amdahl's Law and explain its significance
flashcards.back: Amdahl's Law states that the maximum speedup S ≤ 1/(1-P), where P is the parallel fraction of a program. It quantifies the theoretical speedup limit due to the inherently serial portion of a program.

flashcards.front: What are the two main categories of cache coherence protocols?
flashcards.back: Snooping-based protocols (each cache monitors bus transactions) and Directory-based protocols (central directory maintains coherence information). Used in shared memory multiprocessors.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of an interconnection network in parallel systems?
flashcards.back: Interconnection networks provide communication pathways between processors, memory, and I/O devices. Key metrics include bandwidth, latency, bisection bandwidth, and diameter, which affect overall system performance.
